data.title: Fandango and TikTok team up to sell movie tickets
data.description:
Thanks to a tie-up with Fandango (NASDAQ:CMCSA), TikTok users can now purchase theater tickets directly on the social media app.

Tickets can be purchased through TikTok Spotlight, an integration that was launched by the site last year to highlight new movies and TV shows. By selecting the “Get Tickets” option on the TikTok site, users can select viewing times and purchase seats for their favorite releases.

Users can buy tickets beginning September 4 on select theatrical titles, starting with Disney’s (DIS) upcoming Tron: Ares with a nationwide release on October 10.

The collaboration between the Chinese platform and the leading online movie ticketer was “a powerful step in meeting audiences in their daily digital lives, making it easier to turn the excitement of discovering a movie into the instant gratification of securing your tickets in advance, with no lines and no waiting,” said Fandango’s SVP of marketing and partnerships, Claire Ripsteen.

data.title: House lawmakers probe CVS over bill lobbying in Louisiana
data.description:
Two Republican members of the House of Representatives are looking into allegations that CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) used confidential patient information as part of a lobbying effort in Louisiana to stop the passage of a bill in the state that would prevent pharmacy benefit managers from also owning local pharmacies.

House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform Chairman James Comer (Ky.) and Subcommittee on Federal Law Enforcement Chairman Clay Higgins (La.) are pressing CVS CEO David Joyner to see if the company used a text message campaign that apparently contained patient information as well as warnings that the Louisiana legislation, House Bill 358, could shut down pharmacies and increase prescription costs. The messages were sent to CVS customers.

CVS operates 119 pharmacies in the state. The company also owns the PBM CVS Caremark.

"This apparent conflict of interest is problematic because CVS Caremark manages prescription drug plans for thousands of Louisianians and also contracts with the [Office of Group Benefits], the insurance provider for Louisiana state employees," the letter reads. "This text message campaign raises ethical and potential legal issues if indeed CVS Pharmacy used confidential patient information, obtained through a state contract, to lobby against H.B. 358.

"The inflammatory and misleading text messages—which included threats of pharmacy location closures, increased prescription costs, and loss of service providers—sought to encourage CVS Pharmacy customers to contact Louisiana lawmakers to oppose the bill."

Comer and Higgins have asked Joyner for documents and communications related to CVS' use of customers' personal information for use in political advocacy or lobbying. 

A request for comment to CVS has yet to be returned to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Fed's soft-landing bid faces test in August jobs report, with revisions looming
data.description:
The big jobs report due Friday is expected to show a "fairly steady" unemployment rate, capped by U.S. President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, even with cooling job gains in the face of a low hiring, low firing environment. 

On average, economists expect nonfarm payrolls of just 74K in August, slightly above the prior month's 73K, and see the jobless rate inching up to 4.3% from 4.2% in July.

The Federal Reserve will continue to keep a close eye on the unemployment rate, in particular, as Trump's immigration policies limit labor supply, said Glassdoor Economist Daniel Zhao. Healthcare payroll gains -- among one of the largest contributors to overall employment -- will be another indicator worth watching.

"If we start to see a slowdown in healthcare, that really does raise some red flag about the sustainability of our jobs growth streak that we're on when the rest of the economy is so sluggish," Zhao said in a virtual interview with Seeking Alpha.

The big tell will be revisions. As in previous months, large revisions to earlier payroll figures could reshape the picture and spell trouble for the U.S. central bank's soft-landing efforts. With a labor market already slowing and inflation risks tilted higher in the face of tariff uncertainty, "there is less margin for error," Zhao said.

Markets remain confident the Fed will start cutting rates when it meets later in mid-September, following the last month's weak jobs report and Chair Jerome Powell's dovish shift at Jackson Hole. "If the August jobs report shows hiring is still weak and slack in the job market is widening, it will probably clinch the case for a rate cut at the September decision," said Comerica Chief Economist Bill Adams.

St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem noted this week that recent economic data reinforced his view of downside risks to the labor market, and warned that benchmark revisions in the upcoming jobs report "may reveal an even lower pace of recent payroll growth." Fresh data out Thursday showed more signs of slowing in the labor market, with private sector employment rising less than expected in August and job cuts amounting to the highest year-to-date total since 2020.

May and June payrolls both experienced outsized revisions in last month's report, signaling that job creation in the U.S. was weaker than previously estimated, and "we shouldn't be surprised if we see large revisions for June and July as well" in Friday's report, Zhao said.

He added that with June's hiring so "close to the edge," a downward revision could easily turn those gains into losses.

Note the annual revision of the Bureau of Labor Statistics' payrolls benchmark is slated for release next week -- making it the last major jobs data before the Federal Open Market Committee gathers -- and it could reveal that the seasonally adjusted level of nonfarm payrolls for March 2025 was 475K-790K lower than reported, according to Wells Fargo economists. Evercore ISI expects the level to drop by ~625K.

Meanwhile, wage growth is expected to remain elevated, though it's not "sending warning signals as an inflationary factor," Zhao said, noting "it's fairly close to where it was before the pandemic." With most signals pointing to a cooling job market, he doubts the Fed will lean too heavily on wages to delay rate cuts.

Average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% M/M in August, holding steady from July's pace, per the average economist estimate. Average weekly hours are also expected to stall at 34.3.

"The problem for the Fed would be if the labor shortage leads to higher wages, in which case the Fed could be actually forced to hike interest rates," said SA analyst Damir Tokic. "Thus, the focus is on wage growth," he contended.

SA Editor Liz Kiesche contributed to this story.

data.title: US SEC unveils agenda to revamp crypto policies
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Thursday unveiled its agenda, which could see proposals to revamp regulations related to cryptocurrency and ease Wall Street rules.

The SEC outlined several initiatives covering cryptocurrency policies, which were last reviewed or updated by SEC Chair Paul Atkins on September 4, 2025. The agenda covers proposing rules related to the offer and sale of crypto assets to help clarify the regulatory framework.

“This regulatory agenda reflects that it is a new day at the Securities and Exchange Commission,” Atkins said, adding that the items on the agenda “represent the Commission's renewed focus on supporting innovation, capital formation, market efficiency, and investor protection.”

The SEC considers covering deregulatory rule proposals to reduce compliance burdens to simplify capital raising and investor access to private businesses. The SEC said it would consider amending existing rules to improve and modernize them, while addressing disclosure burdens.

“Importantly, the agenda reflects our withdrawal of a host of items from the last Administration that do not align with the goal that regulation should be smart, effective, and appropriately tailored within the confines of our statutory authority,” the SEC added.

If enacted, the new policies could be a win for the crypto industry, which has been actively pushing for clear rules to normalize its adoption with traditional finance.

Earlier in July, U.S. President Donald Trump’s crypto working group called on financial regulators to take action and draft new crypto-specific rules. It has also called on the SEC to allow digital assets to trade at the federal level.

Trump had formed the Working Group on Digital Asset Markets to establish a comprehensive regulatory regime for digital assets and make the U.S. the "crypto capital of the world."

data.title: NY Fed's Williams expects moving rates closer to neutral will be appropriate
data.description:
New York Fed President John Williams said on Thursday he expects that it will soon be appropriate to move the Federal Reserve's policy rate toward what's considered a neutral stance — that is, where interest rates neither stimulate nor drag on the economy.

"Looking ahead, if progress on our dual mandate goals continues as in my baseline forecast, I anticipate it will become appropriate to move interest rates toward a more neutral stance over time," he said in prepared text for his opening remarks at the Economic Club of New York.

Currently, the federal funds rate target range is 4.25%-4.50%. The continued gradual cooling in the labor market leads him to believe that monetary policy is being "modestly restrictive."

It's a delicate balance, he said, "On the one hand, we need to keep the labor market in balance to ensure that the effects of tariffs do not spill over into a longer-lasting broad increase in inflation. On the other hand, maintaining a stance of 'too restrictive policy for too long' could increase risks to our maximum employment mandate."

Assessing the rate of job growth is being complicated by recent sharp changes in labor supply, Williams said. "I therefore put greater weight on other indicators of the level of labor market conditions in assessing the strength of the labor market."

Assessing the price stability side of the Fed's mandate is also made more challenging by the tariffs imposed in recent month. "The realized aggregate effects of tariffs so far have not been as large as expected earlier in the year, but it’s still early days, and it will take time for them to come to be fully realized," he said. 

Overall, he expects tariffs to increase overall prices by a total of 1%-1.5%, with effects continuing through the first half of 2026.

Regarding the U.S. economy, he expects 2025 GDP growth to be 1.25%-1.50% and PCE inflation to rise to 3%-3.25% this year then decline to ~2.5% next year.  Williams expects the unemployment rate to gradually rise to ~4.5% next year.

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Mazda reports decline in August U.S. sales
data.description:
• Mazda North American Operations (OTCPK:MZDAY) (OTCPK:MZDAF) reported August U.S. sales of 38,140 vehicles, down 7.6% from a year earlier.
  • On a daily selling rate basis, sales declined 4.1%, with 27 selling days this year versus 28 in August 2024.
  • Sales rose 3.5% to 293,495 vehicles, YTD. 
  • Certified pre-owned [CPO] sales fell 4% in August to 7,212 units, with year-to-date CPO sales down 1% at 50,550.
  • The CX-90 posted record August sales of 6,801 units, while the CX-50 recorded its second-best August with 10,028 units sold.
  • Mazda Canada sales declined 2% year-over-year in August to 8,274 vehicles but rose 15.7% year-to-date. Sales in Mexico fell 6% to 7,927 units in August, with year-to-date sales up 10% at 67,639.
  • Source Press Release

data.title: Medicare to expand catastrophic coverage eligibility
data.description:
The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) announced on Thursday it will broaden access to catastrophic health coverage through a new hardship exemption guidance, expected to roll out with the start of open enrollments on Nov. 1.

The exemption, designed as a response to potential premium hikes in 2026 will be available for those who are ineligible for advance payments of the premium tax credit or cost-sharing reductions based on their projected annual household income.

“Through these efforts, more Americans will be able to qualify for catastrophic health coverage based on need, beginning November 1st with the start of open enrollment,” the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services added.

While catastrophic plans typically with lower monthly premiums offer 10 essential health benefits like other marketplace plans, they cover three primary care visits before deductibles kick in, giving Americans a more affordable way to obtain health insurance.

Operators in the ACA marketplace include Centene (NYSE:CNC), Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH), UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), Oscar Health (NYSE:OSCR), Elevance Health (ELV), CVS Health (CVS), and Cigna (CI).

data.title: Vale reopens key mine, plans to spend $12B in Brazil's Minas Gerais
data.description:
Vale (NYSE:VALE) reopened its Capanema iron ore mine, which had been inactive for 22 years, and said it plans to spend 67B reais (~$12.3B) in its mining operations in Brazil's Minas Gerais state by 2030, Reuters reported Thursday.

Vale (NYSE:VALE) said most of the spending will be focused on expanding filtering and dry stacking of iron ore tailings, as it seeks to reduce the use of tailings dams in its local operations to 20% from 30%, a company goal since the collapse of two dams in Minas Gerais in 2015 and 2019, which killed hundreds of people and caused severe environmental damage.

The miner said the Capanema mine will now operate without using water in processing, generating no tailings and removing the need for dams.

"Capanema exemplifies the new phase of mining in Minas Gerais and reinforces our commitment to a more responsible production process," Vale (VALE) CEO Gustavo Pimenta told Reuters.

The mine will add ~15M metric tons/year to Vale's (VALE) iron ore output and help the company achieve its goal of producing 340M-360M tons next year, the company said.
